title: Banks face big AI-led risks: APRA
full_text:
The banking regulator says the Trump administration's decision to shut off global access to Anthropic's most powerful artificial intelligence model has created significant risks for Australia's major banks, and it wants the biggest lenders to share access to their most sophisticated cybersecurity tools with smaller rivals to ensure broad defences across the economy.
In the agency's second major speech on AI-powered cyber threats in as many weeks, Australian Prudential Regulation Authority executive board member Therese McCarthy Hockey said there was no doubt that Australia was "entering a dangerous period in the AI revolution".
APRA, "on the good authority of our security agency peers, is confident that frontier AI presents a paradigm shift," she said on Wednesday. The comments come weeks after banks said they were concerned about the ability of next-generation AI models, such as Anthropic's Mythos, to compromise cybersecurity systems and began efforts to mitigate against those issues.
Anthropic said in April that it would not publicly release Mythos, citing the cybersecurity risks, given that the model is adept at identifying unknown flaws in defences, putting the entire global financial system at risk.
Anthropic had been preparing to expand Project Glasswing to include Australian banks, allowing them to patch weaknesses before similar models get into the hands of criminal groups. Mythos and similar tools have been able to find vulnerabilities in operating systems that have been there for decades, raising fears they could seriously harm big banks.
Last week, APRA chairman John Lonsdale and Reserve Bank of Australia assistant governor Brad Jones used coordinated speeches at a banking event in Melbourne to place the sector on a war footing, detailing higher expectations on defending the financial system from risks beyond hacking, including disinformation campaigns and insider threats to steal information. Banks were told to step up the vetting of senior staff.
Anthropic said three weeks ago that it would extend access to its Mythos model to Australian companies. Within days, the Trump administration ordered it to suspend foreign access to its advanced models, including an adaptation of Mythos called Fable, citing national security concerns.
Anthropic provided briefings about Mythos to Treasury, the RBA and the stewards of the country's critical infrastructure assets, known collectively as the Systems of National Significance, while it held back access. Anthropic began its staggered launch of Mythos on April 7, when it provided access to Wall Street and technology firms as part of Project Glasswing.
"APRA doesn't need to take a view on the merits of that decision to draw clear lessons from it," McCarthy Hockey said in her speech on Wednesday.
"For Australian financial institutions, frontier AI is not just a cyber-risk issue. It's third-party risk, a concentration risk and a sovereign access risk. A critical business process, control or cyber-defence capability that depends on a single offshore frontier AI model may be disrupted not only by an outage or cyber incident but by a regulatory decision made overseas."
McCarthy Hockey said cybersecurity should not be seen as a competitive advantage, but one demanding an industry-wide response, given the "successful penetration of an entity can also allow sophisticated actors to use these digital connections as a backdoor into other targets".
"In an environment where an attack on one of us could be an attack on any of us, our financial system is only as resilient to cyberattacks as the weakest link in the chain," she said in her speech to a conference on Wednesday.
APRA now expects the country's largest banks that have been granted access to frontier AI models to share information and insights with peers and suppliers.
"Where one institution learns something material about AI-enabled vulnerabilities, model limitations, jailbreak techniques or defensive use cases, the system as a whole benefits when that knowledge is shared quickly and safely," McCarthy Hockey said in her comments.
In a letter to all banks, sent on April 30, APRA declared that the governance of AI was not keeping pace with the level of adoption. Boards did not have the literacy needed to provide effective challenge on AI-related risks and oversight, and information security practices were struggling to keep pace.
Commonwealth Bank, which has the closest partnerships with the world's two largest AI platform developers, Anthropic and OpenAI, said it was willing to share knowledge in the interests of national security. "Improving and maintaining Australia's cybersecurity requires a whole-of-ecosystem approach and collaboration between government, industry, and the community," said CBA chief security officer Nicola Nicol.
"This involves sharing intelligence, knowledge and insights with government and industry partners on emerging technologies, such as frontier models, to help lift cyber capability across the ecosystem."
Mythos' capabilities are game-changing because it can chain several lower-level vulnerabilities together to create new attack paths to break into bank systems. Those problems will only worsen as more powerful models, including open-source versions made by Chinese developers, are released.
In her speech, McCarthy Hockey also warned that quantum computing could destroy the current encryption standards used to protect digital information, including emails and bank accounts.
While a standard computer today would need about 149 million years of compute power to "brute force" an eight-digit combination password, it's estimated that it would take a quantum computer about 18 minutes. In March, the Global Risk Institute said there was a 50:50 chance a quantum machine would break the algorithms that protected transactions within a decade.
RBA governor Michele Bullock warned about quantum computing security threats last October. Last month, London-based bank HSBC said it was accelerating preparations for quantum technology, including a security uplift to update algorithms that protected payments to new quantum-safe standards. Australian banks have started that work, too.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: James Eyers
heading: Data Science and AI
